coredata.dc:title: Active packaging film based on chitosan/gelatin blend incorporated with mango peel carbon dots: Properties and shelf life extension of minced pork

coredata.dc:description: Active packaging is essential for reducing food quality loss and ensuring consumer safety. Recently, carbon dots, synthesized from agricultural bio-wastes, have been used as active nanofillers. Mango peels, generally discarded as waste, can serve as potential precursor for synthesis of carbon dots. Mango peel carbon dots (MPCD) were prepared and characterized. Characteristics of active film based on chitosan (CS)/fish gelatin (FG) blend incorporated with MPCD at different concentrations (1, 3, and 5 wt%) were investigated. MPCD with augmenting concentrations enhanced mechanical properties of CS/FG film. Film containing 5 % MPCD had 15 % higher tensile strength than the control (without MPCD). The film containing MPCD showed the improved antioxidant activity, antimicrobial and UV barrier properties. The pouch (5 × 5 cm2) made from film added with 5 % MPCD via heat sealing was used for packaging minced pork. Minced pork packed in the pouch showed lower bacterial growth (below 6 log CFU/g) and chemical changes than that packed in polyethylene pouch during 15 days of storage at 4 °C. Therefore, the conversion of mango peel into valuable carbon dots promotes a zero-waste sustainable approach in line with the biocircular economy. Active pouch could be employed as novel biodegradable active and green packaging for the food industry.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Active packaging is essential for reducing food quality loss and ensuring consumer safety. Recently, carbon dots, synthesized from agricultural bio-wastes, have been used as active nanofillers. Mango peels, generally discarded as waste, can serve as potential precursor for synthesis of carbon dots. Mango peel carbon dots (MPCD) were prepared and characterized. Characteristics of active film based on chitosan (CS)/fish gelatin (FG) blend incorporated with MPCD at different concentrations (1, 3, and 5 wt%) were investigated. MPCD with augmenting concentrations enhanced mechanical properties of CS/FG film. Film containing 5 % MPCD had 15 % higher tensile strength than the control (without MPCD). The film containing MPCD showed the improved antioxidant activity, antimicrobial and UV barrier properties. The pouch (5 × 5 cm2) made from film added with 5 % MPCD via heat sealing was used for packaging minced pork. Minced pork packed in the pouch showed lower bacterial growth (below 6 log CFU/g) and chemical changes than that packed in polyethylene pouch during 15 days of storage at 4 °C. Therefore, the conversion of mango peel into valuable carbon dots promotes a zero-waste sustainable approach in line with the biocircular economy. Active pouch could be employed as novel biodegradable active and green packaging for the food industry.

ce:section.ce:para: Food packaging plays a vital role in preserving and maintaining the quality of food products by protecting foods from external factors, thus ensuring their suitability for export, storage, and consumption [1]. In addition, active packaging, a cutting-edge food packaging technology, can regulate the packaging environment or inhibit microorganisms by releasing bioactive compounds or the removal of undesirable substances [2]. Incorporation of bioactive compounds within polymer networks or film is a common technique used in active food packaging [3]. This active packaging method is designed to deliberately incorporate active components that can be released into the food or absorb undesirable substances from the food or its environment in the package [4,5]. Technological advancements are continuously driving the progress of new active packaging systems [6]. As a consequence, higher food safety and food quality maintenance can be gained, while waste or pollution minimization can be achieved.

Currently, natural polymers such as polysaccharides and proteins are gaining much attention in film fabrication because of their biocompatibility, lack of toxicity, ability to degrade naturally and renewability. Among polysaccharides, chitosan (CS) is popular in both lab scale and industry for its excellent structural and functional groups making it suitable for food packaging [7]. Additionally, proteins are another essential biopolymers with unique properties and yield composite materials with unique properties when combined with polysaccharides [8]. Gelatin, an ionic hydrophilic linear polypeptide, is an abundant, biodegradable and low-cost bio-based material [9]. Blending biopolymers significantly alters the physio-chemical properties of the resulting film. Those changes are governed by compositions, molecular weight, hydration and cross-linking ability of those polymers. The resulting blend films had the improved barrier and functional properties [10]. Among them, CS/fish gelatin (FG)-based films exhibited the excellent barrier and bioactivities such as gas barrier, antioxidative and antibacterial properties [10,11]. CS and FG are blended through simple mixing or chemical crosslinking. These molecular interactions, either covalent bonds or weak interactions and crosslinking between CS and FG polymeric chains, resulting in better network stability, thereby enhancing the mechanical, thermal stability and barrier properties [10]. Moreover, CS/FG blend films extended the shelf life of seafood during storage due to their antimicrobial and antioxidant properties [12].

Nanocomposite materials are formed by mixing active nanomaterials with the film components, purposefully combining them to obtain desirable attributes with tailored properties [13]. The addition of nanoparticles to a polymer matrix could improve mechanical, barrier and functional properties. The interaction between the nanoparticle and the polymer happens through covalent bonding and/or weak interactions [14]. The bonds formed between the active compounds and the film matrix in the packaging materials regulate the release and migration of molecules and are also controlled by the porosity, swelling, and biodegradation of the film [15].

Recently, the CS/FG blend films have gained significant attention in producing nanocomposites [16]. These nanocomposites are formed by adding nanoparticles to the CS/FG matrix, significantly improving the film properties. Carbon dots (CDs), smaller than 10 nm, are promising fillers due to their high water solubility, low cost, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and environmental sustainability [17]. The hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups present on the surface of the CDs exhibit excellent properties, including fluorescence, UV-barrier, antibacterial, and antioxidant properties, making them ideal for food preservation [18]. CDs interact strongly and adsorb well to biological surfaces due to their size and surface functional groups [17]. Mango peel is generally discarded as waste, however they are rich in bioactive compounds such as polyphenols, carotenoids, gallic acid, flavonoids, mangiferin, and dietary fiber [19]. CDs from agricultural waste or sources such as banana peel and green tea have been incorporated into packaging, in which active green packings have been successfully developed. Nonetheless, mango peel-derived carbon dots (MPCD) remain non-exploited. By addressing this gap, the present study not only demonstrated the bioactivities of MPCD but also promoted waste valorization, aligning with global sustainability goal. More importantly, active green packaging could be produced for food preservation or shelf life extension.

This research intended to create and characterize MPCD and to formulate an active packaging film based on a CS/FG blend incorporated with MPCD at varying levels. Additionally, the quality of minced pork packaged in a pouch made from the developed film during refrigerated storage was assessed.

Fish gelatin powder (200 Bloom, Type A) was purchased from Gel-Tec Co. Ltd. (Seoul, South Korea). Chitosan powder (MW: 1.9–3.1× 105 Da, degree of deacetylation: 75–85 %), Chemicals for antioxidant assays were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Mangoes and minced pork were brought from a nearby market (Seoul, Korea).

Nam Dok Mai mangoes (Magnifera indica L) were peeled and the peels were washed twice with tap water and finally rinsed with distilled water. The peeled skins were dried overnight at 60 °C for 12 h in a hot air oven. The dried samples were ground using a blender for 10 min to obtain the fine powder and were sieved to obtain a particle size <300 μm.

MPCD were prepared by a hydrothermal process [20]. The dried mango peel (MP) powder (2 %, w/v) was suspended in distilled water. The suspension was placed in a stainless reactor containing a Teflon-lined chamber. The reactor was subsequently heated at 200 °C in a muffle furnace for 6 h. Thereafter, the reactor was allowed to cool down to room temperature with the aid of electric fan. The resulting yellow-brown solution containing MPCD was filtered through a 100 μm Whatman filter paper to remove large particulates and other impurities. Thereafter, the filtrate was subjected to filtration using a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain nano-sized particles. The obtained MPCD were then freeze-dried and stored in an air-tight zip lock bag at 4 °C for further use and characterization. The yield of MPCD was approximately 23.35 % (w/w), calculated based on the initial weight of the MP used.

Light absorption in the range of 200–800 nm of MPCD dispersion (50 ppm concentration) was measured with the aid of a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea). MPCD were also subjected to Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic analysis (FTIR, IFS 66 V/S, Bruker, Ettlingen, Germany). The absorption data were collected in 32 scans in the wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1 with an ATR platinum crystal at 25 °C.

The size and shape of the synthesized MPCD were determined using a transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEM-2100F, Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence responses of the MPCD (50 ppm concentration) were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fluoromax, Horiba Inc., Park Avenue, Edison, NJ), respectively.

The radical scavenging activities (RSA) of MPCD toward ABTS and DPPH radicals were examined as described by Khan, Ezati, Kim and Rhim [21]. The activities of different MPCD solutions at varying concentrations (10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 ppm) were analyzed and expressed as % radical scavenged.

The antimicrobial activity of MPCD against various bacteria, including Listeria monocytogenes (LM), Staphylococcus aureus (SA), Streptococcus entericus (SE), and Escherichia coli (EC) was measured using agar well diffusion method [11].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of active packaging films using CS/FG blend containing MPCD
ce:section.ce:para: CS (2 %, w/v) was firstly dissolved in 1 % (v/v) acetic acid using the magnetic stirrer for 6 h. FG (2 %, w/v) was dissolved in distilled water and stirred at 70 °C until fully dissolved. CS/FG blend was prepared by mixing CS and FG solution at a 1:1 ratio (v/v) and stirred for 2 h at 50 °C to obtain a homogenous blend. The glycerol (30 % w/w, based on polymer) was incorporated into the polymer solution as a plasticizer, enhancing film flexibility. Based on the solid weight of CS/FG, MPCD at varying concentrations (0, 1, 3, and 5 wt%) were mixed with the film-forming solution, stirred (50 °C, 1 h) and sonicated in a sonication bath for 15 min to achieve a homogeneous dispersion. The film-forming solution was then evenly cast onto well balanced Teflon-lined glass plates (30 × 24 cm2) to form a uniform layer. The films were dried at room temperature (23 ± 2 °C) for 2 days. After that, the dried films were then manually peeled and placed in a humidity chamber (50 ± 1 % RH and 25 ± 1 °C) for 48 h. The fabricated films were named CS/FG for CS and FG blend film (control) and CS/FG-MPCD1%, CS/FG-MPCD3%, and CS/FG-MPCD5% for CS/FG containing MPCD at 1 %, 3 %, and 5 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Film thickness, mechanical and barrier properties
ce:section.ce:para: Thickness, tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EAB) and water vapor permeability of films were examined as outlined in our previous paper [10].

Water contact angle (WCA) representing surface hydrophobicity of the films was assessed using a contact angle analyzer (Phoenix 150, Surface Electro-Optics Co., Ltd., South Korea). Film samples (2.5 × 10 cm2) were secured onto a movable platform. A 10 μL-droplet of distilled water was dispensed onto the surface of the film using a micro syringe. When the droplet was stabilized, the WCA was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color, transmission value, opacity and UV barrier properties
ce:section.ce:para: The color of film samples was measured using a Konica Minolta chromameter (CR-400 Chromameter, Tokyo, Japan) [22]. The values were expressed as L*, a*, b* and ΔE.

The light transmittance and UV barrier properties of film samples were measured using the method of Ezati and Rhim [23]. The light transmission of films was assessed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, across a wavelength range of 200–800 nm.

The surface and cross-section morphologies, along with the EDX (elemental mapping) of the MPCD-incorporated films, were determined by a field emission-scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, SU 8010, Hitachi Co., Ltd., S4 Matsuda, Japan) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) (S.No. 56756, Oxford instruments INCA, UK) at 8 kV accelerating voltage. An acceleration voltage of 20 kV with 10 k magnification was employed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopic and XPS spectra
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of all the films was collected as described in previous section 2.3.1. The acquired spectra were normalized to ensure reliable comparisons between samples.

The chemical structure and surface functional groups of the films were also determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co. Ltd., Waltham, MA, USA).

Antioxidant activities of the films were evaluated using the ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging assays, following the procedures described in section 2.3.2. Prior to testing, the film samples (100 mg) were cut into fine pieces using scissors and added directly to the assay solution (10 mL) containing DPPH and ABTS radicals, vortexed and incubated for 30 min. Finally, the reaction mixtures were filtered using Whatman filter paper no. 4. The absorbance of filtrates was measured in comparison with the solution (without film sample), and % radical scavenged was reported.

Antimicrobial properties of the films were assessed against EC and LM using the real-time kill kinetics analysis as outlined by Roy, Ezati and Rhim [24]. Briefly, film samples were exposed to UV irradiation for 20 min before the analysis. The sterilized film (100 mg) was suspended in 20 mL of medium broth containing the target microorganism at 1 × 106 CFU/mL. The total viable count (TVC) was determined after incubation at 37 °C for 18–24 h. Incubation was computed and reported as cell viability (log CFU/mL).

The formulated films were sealed to make a pouch (50 × 50 mm2). Minced pork (5 g) was packaged in pouches made from different films. The air in the pouch was excluded manually before being sealed using a heat sealer. In order to compare the formed active packaging films with conventional packaging, samples were packaged in commercially available polyethylene (PE) pouch of the same dimensions. All pouches containing minced pork were sealed and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator. Throughout the storage period, the samples were randomly taken for quality analyses at 3-day intervals up to 15 days.

pH and thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) values of the minced pork were determined at 3-day intervals. The pH of the minced pork was measured using a pH meter (Thermo Scientific, A-211-pH meter, Indonesia). To determine the lipid oxidation of samples, TBARS value was measured using TBA reagent as described by Benjakul and Bauer [25] and values was expressed as mg malondialdehyde (MDA)/kg sample. Minced pork samples were evaluated for TVC and psychrophilic bacterial count (PBC) following the procedure of Olatunde, Benjakul and Vongkamjan [26]. TVC and PBC were used as standard techniques to assess the bacterial load in the stored pork samples. The meat samples (5 g) were homogenized with 45 mL of 0.1 % peptone water using a stomacher (BagMixer® 400, Interscience, France) for 180 s. Subsequently, 1 mL of serially diluted homogenized aliquots were spread on 3 M™ Petrifilm counting plates. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h for TVC and 4 °C for 7 days for PBC, the colonies were counted, and the microbial load was reported as Log CFU/g.

The entire study was conducted using a completely randomized design (CRD). Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to evaluate the significant variation between the treatments. Duncan's Multiple Range Test was conducted to compare the means across the samples with a significant level of P < 0.05 using SPSS software (Statistical Package for Social Science, IBM software, USA). The results were reported as mean ± standard deviation (SD).

MPCD displayed a major peak around 280 nm in the absorption spectrum, corresponding to π-π*transitions (C=C) within the CD structure (Fig. 1a). This finding was consistent with the previously documented CDs from green tea by Khan, Ezati and Rhim [20]. These peaks are attributed to specific electronic transitions within the CD structure, which indicates the electronic configuration and nature of the energy levels within the carbon dots. This is a characteristic feature of carbon-based materials where π-electron systems are present [24]. The MPCD emitted a strong blue fluorescence under UV light exposure (inset of Fig. 1a), which indicated that MPCD could absorb UV light and emit in the visible range. Smaller CDs often emit blue fluorescence due to their quantum confinement effects, which results in a higher energy band gap [27]. Blue emission can reflect specific functional groups that stabilize the higher energy emission. This blue color emission is a characteristic feature of CDs from biomass-based CDs, and the emission intensity can be influenced by the synthesis method and starting materials [28]. Similar to other CDs from different sources, MPCD also exhibited strong absorption in the UV region compared to the visible region [29].

The characteristic IR-spectrum proved the presence of surface functional groups on MPCD in comparison with the MP (Fig. 1b). The MP showed characteristic peaks at 3330 cm−1, assigned to N-H/O-H stretching vibration and other absorption bands were observed at 2938 cm−1 (CH symmetric stretching), 1729 cm−1 (C=O), 1627 cm−1 (C=N; Schiff base), 1145 cm−1 (CN stretching) and 1012 cm−1 (CO group) [30]. The MPCD showed peaks at 3331 cm−1 and 2922 cm-1, corresponding to N-H/O-H stretching vibration and CH symmetric stretching, respectively. The 3331 cm−1 peak of the MPCD corresponded to the polar functional groups, which signifies the hydrophilic nature of the CDs [31]. Strong absorption peaks at 1664 and 1606 cm−1, ascribing to CO and CC stretching, respectively. The IR spectra showed a slight peak intensity at 1606 cm−1, corresponding to CC groups in MPCD, while no peak was found in MP. This could be plausibly due to the molecular alterations in MP after the hydrothermal reaction, in which the CC groups were predominant. Other peaks, such as 1519 cm−1, 1188 cm−1, and 1018 cm-1, represent NH bending CN and CC stretching vibrations, respectively [32]. The peaks around 768 cm−1 corresponded to the aromatic ring's bending vibration in and out plane [32]. The structural deformation of the MPCD at the molecular level was more likely responsible for their enhanced functional properties [33].

TEM was used to visualize the surface morphology and size of synthesized MPCD (Fig. 1c). TEM images revealed that MPCD were well dispersed, quasi spherical in structure without apparent aggregation. Based on the histogram (Fig. 1c, inset), the size of MPCD was heterogenous and average particle size was mostly observed in the range of 8 to 10 nm. The TEM pictures illustrated that the size distribution of the particles was within the quantum range, as stated before [28].

Fluorescent emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths from 270 to 500nm with an increment of 10nm were recorded at RT (Fig. 1d). At an excitation wavelength of 340nm, the MPCD had a strong fluorescent peak in the 350–600nm range, and the maximum intensity was observed at 416nm (Fig. 1d). When excited at 416 nm, the emission spectrum showed higher intensity at 429 nm (Fig. 1d, inset). Moreover, the intensity of the emission wavelength shifted to higher wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increased from 360 to 450nm, therefore showing a steady red shift in the emission peaks [34]. The emission behavior of MPCD is probably caused by various factors, such as the carbon source used for synthesis, size and shape variations, and the existence of diverse functional groups on the surface of CD [20].

Antioxidant abilities of the MPCD toward ABTS and DPPH radicals were upsurged with rising concentrations of MPCD (P < 0.05) (Fig. 2a). The presence of surface functionalized groups on the CDs, as confirmed by FTIR spectra, could plausibly participate in radical scavenging activity (RSA) to generate more stable products [35]. ABTS-RSA was significantly higher than that of DPPH-RSA when the same concentration of MPCD was used (P < 0.05). This could be due to the hydrophilic nature and better dissolution in the solution due to the presence of surface OH groups of the MPCD in distilled water than in methanol, as evidenced by high ABTS-RSA [36]. Almost 100 % ABTS was scavenged with MPCD at a concentration of 75 ppm.

The antimicrobial activity of MPCD was tested against four bacteria (SA, LM, SE, and EC) through the agar well diffusion method. MPCD showed a higher diameter of inhibition zone against SA and LM, compared to SE and EC (Fig. 2b and c). From the above observations, the diameter of inhibition zone was greater for Gram-positive bacteria than Gram-negative bacteria. This was likely owing to the variances in bacterial cell wall composition and affinity to bind with MPCD between those two types of bacteria. Gram-positive bacteria possess a relatively simple peptidoglycan layer with exposed peptide sequences, while Gram-negative bacteria have a complex-layered peptidoglycan layer in their cell wall sandwiched between two cell membranes made up of lipopolysaccharides [37]. The small size of the MPCD helps in penetration into the cell wall of bacteria more profoundly in Gram-positive bacteria. In the case of Gram-negative bacteria, the presence of a lipophilic membrane of the cell wall may hinder the diffusion or penetration of MPCD due to its hydrophobicity nature, which was evidenced by lower diameter of inhibition zone. Antimicrobial activity of MPCD might be associated with the inhibition of enzyme synthesis and disruption of vital cellular processes, therefore leading to cell death [35].

Additionally, CDs having smaller size could easily penetrate the cell wall and interfere with the essential cellular components, involving protein and nucleic acid synthesis [38,39]. In addition, CDs could cause the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and harm the cellular components, eventually resulting in cell death [40,41]. The antimicrobial activity of MPCD was consistent with CDs from glucose prepared by Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi and Han [42].

The thickness of active packaging films is a crucial factor in determining the mechanical strength, solubility and barrier properties [43]. The thickness of the CS/FG films increased upon incorporation of MPCD (P < 0.05) (Table 1 ). However, no difference in thickness was observed between films with different MPCD levels, which might be due to the small size of MPCD that could be uniformly distributed throughout the CS/FG film matrix [44].

The TS and EAB of the CS/FG blend were 71.06 MPa and 21.77 %, respectively, which were significantly higher than those of monolayer CS and FG films reported in previous studies [11]. This improvement was attributed by the blending of CS and FG, which enhanced both tensile strength and elasticity of the films. TS and EAB were affected to different degrees upon incorporation of MPCD in CS/FG film, probably due to the MPCD distribution and their compatibility within the polymeric matrix. Film incorporated with MPCD at 3 % and 5 % displayed a significant increase in TS compared to the control (P < 0.05). Film containing 5 % MPCD had a maximum TS of 81.74 MPa (Table 1). However, the EAB of the films was reduced with the incorporation of MPCD (P < 0.05). No difference in the EAB of the films with the varied concentration of MPCD was found (P > 0.05). The results indicated that MPCD in the studied range could reinforce the network of CS/FG films, especially at 5 %, possibly through the hydrogen bonds. These hydrogen bonds created a stronger and more cohesive structure, making the film more resistant to breakage under tension. The compatible interaction between CS, FG and MPCD could also favor the ordered arrangement of the film network. As a consequence, a strong and stable network was created, thus enhancing the tensile force resistance with reduced flexibility of the film [36]. These findings were in tandem with those results of CS/FG film incorporated with green tea carbon dots, in which TS rose but EAB declined [20]. The inclusion of MPCD as nanofillers higher than a certain level increased mechanical rigidity, depending on their distribution in the film network [45].

The MPCD-incorporated CS/FG films had no significant difference in WVP regarding the amount of MPCD added (P > 0.05) (Table 1). This demonstrated that the quantum-sized CDs were unable to fill the space between polymeric chains, even at higher levels. Hampering by a dense network imparted fewer water molecules to pass through the film.

The WCA of CS/FG film was 60.04°, and the WCA of the CS/FG films upsurged after the incorporation of MPCD, and the extent of the increase was proportional to the amount of CD incorporation. This was most likely due to the hydrogen bond between MPCD rich in OH groups and film components. Less availability of -OH groups in the polymeric film network and the presence of aromatic carbon structures on the MPCD resulted in increased hydrophobicity and higher WCA values [46]. It was anticipated that film incorporated with nanofillers would have enhanced surface hydrophobicity [47].

CS/FG film was slightly yellowish with a lightness value (L*) of 89.77, which coincided with that reported for CS/FG film [20]. However, the L* values dropped considerably for the films containing MPCD (P < 0.05), and the value decreased with augmenting MPCD concentrations. The yellowish color was uniform throughout the film, which indicated that CS, FG and MPCD were homogenously distributed throughout the film. The a* value of the CS/FG film incorporated with MPCD upsurged up to 13.26 for a film containing 5 % MPCD, whereas the control film had a* value of 2.73 (P < 0.05). The b* value of the film increased drastically upon the incorporation of MPCD because of the tan color of CDs. The ΔE* of the films rose from 6.57 to 22.96–53.71 with the incorporation of MPCD. Khan, Priyadarshi, Bhattacharya and Rhim [36] observed a similar pattern of ΔE*, which rose to 50.3 from 6.2 for the CDs incorporated FG-carrageenan composite films. Visual views of different films are given in Fig. 3a. CS/FG film was slightly yellowish, while the CDs incorporated films became more yellowish-brown due to the yellow color of MPCD. The film incorporated with MPCD5% had a more intense dark color compared to others. Generally, color was uniform with all the film formulations, suggesting that MPCD were homogenously spread throughout the film.

UV and visible light transmission of various films are depicted in Fig. 3b. In the visible region, CS/FG film showed high transparency with a light transmittance of over 90 %. However, all the films showed good UV-blocking abilities associated with the existence of aromatic amino acids in the FG [48]. Only CS/FG film had some UV-C transmission, while other films completely blocked the UV-B and UV-C transmission. Light transmittance further decreased upon incorporation of MPCD in a concentration-dependent manner. UV-blocking abilities of MPCD could prevent light transmission of the films in the UV-B region by 99 % (Table. 2 ). MPCD-incorporated films showed better UV-blocking ability compared to CS/FG film (P < 0.05). UV-A blocking ability of the CS/FG films increased with rising concentration of MPCD (P < 0.05), while MPCD5% incorporated films showed the highest blocking (98.53 %). No difference in UVB blocking (99 %) was observed with varied levels of MPCD. Previously, Khan, Priyadarshi, Bhattacharya and Rhim [36] observed ∼90.2 % of UV-A blocking and 99.7 % of UV-B blocking of CS/FG films incorporated with green tea CDs. This showed that the addition of MPCD in the CS/FG film matrix showed remarkable UV barrier properties [49]. The films containing MPCD were slightly opaque and significantly reduced light transmission in the visible region than CS/FG films (Table 2). Moreover, with an upsurging concentration of MPCD (1–5 %), the opacity value increased (P < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FE-SEM images and FTIR spectra of MPCD-incorporated CS/FG films
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and cross-section structures of CS/FG films with varying levels of MPCD are displayed in Fig. 4a and b, respectively. The surface of the films had a smooth, homogeneous, continuous, intact and crack-free surface morphology. The SEM images demonstrated the compatibility of MPCD with film components, and MPCD were dispersed uniformly across the film's surface (Fig. 4a). The cross-sectional images of CS/FG film showed small voids in their structure. However, the MPCD-incorporated films had no voids or ruptures, demonstrating the compatibility and complexation of CS, FG, and MPCD at the molecular level (Fig. 4b). Apparently, there were no visible gaps, and the film surface exhibited exemplary integrity. Hydrogen bonds might be formed between -OH groups of MPCD, glycerol and CS/FG [36,50]. Therefore, the inclusion of MPCD could enhance the stability and compactness of CS/FG films.

FTIR spectra of CS/FG, CS/FG-MPCD1%, CS/FG-MPCD3%, and CS/FG-MPCD5% films could verify the bonding between the functional groups of MPCD and the polymers, CS and FG (Fig. 4c). Similar to our previous studies, the CS spectrum showed characteristics peaks at characteristic wavenumbers of 3367, 2904, 1604, 1370, 1154, and 1604 cm−1, ascribed to NH and OH stretching, CH stretching, CO stretching of the amide bond, CH3 stretching, asymmetric stretching of C-O-C and CO stretching of C3 carbon, respectively [10]. FTIR of FG showed broad characteristics of a major peak around 3300 to 3400 cm−1, attributing to overlapped OH and NH groups, and a high-intensity peak around 1038 cm−1 represented the CO group [51,52]. The peaks around 1608 and 1394 cm−1 were ascribed to CO stretching and aromatic alkanes, respectively [51]. All the spectra showed the distinctive absorption peaks of FG powder (FG-P) around 1656, 1550, and 1245 cm−1, corresponding to the amide I (CO stretching), II and III (CN stretching, and NH bending) of polypeptide chains, respectively [52,53]. The peaks around 1060 to 1070 cm−1 were ascribed to the CO stretching vibrations [54]. The slight variation in the amplitude of peaks was seen in IR spectra at lower wavenumbers associated with amino and carbonyl moieties after the addition of MPCD. This suggested that electrostatic interactions were associated between these groups [55]. Moreover, the FTIR spectra of MPCD-incorporated films had a broader peak around 3500–3200 cm-1, and slight shifts of the peak were found, suggesting the interaction between some functional groups in CS/FG film. These observations suggested that CS/FG-MPCD complex formation was influenced by interactions between the OH groups of CS/FG and MPCD, either van der Waals interactions or H-bonds [56]. The FTIR spectrum was similar to that of the CS/FG film, and all the spectra had the same characteristic peaks except for a minor shift in position and amplitude of peaks upon incorporation of MPCD. The results confirmed the interaction of MPCD in the CS/FG complex. Following the incorporation of MPCD, the FTIR spectra showed the overlapping of CH, -OH, CC, and CO stretching vibrations and the intensity increased with rising concentration of MPCD in the wavenumber range of 1600 to 800 cm−1 (Fig. 4c). Moreover, the changes in amplitude of peaks in the spectra of the film with a minor shift in the frequency region indicated that the uniformly distributed MPCD could interact with CS/FG via weak bonds mostly hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interaction in the film matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS and EDX spectra of MPCD incorporated CS/FG films
ce:section.ce:para: The bonding, arrangement and chemical composition of CS/FG and CS/FG-MPCD5% films were determined from the XPS pattern. As anticipated for the fabricated films, XPS spectra showed the major constitutional elements of the composite matrices (Fig. 5 a-d). The spectra revealed the occurrence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen as indicated by the binding energies at 284.82, 399.37, and 532.51 eV for CS/FG film and 284.78, 398.22, and 531.28 eV for CS/FG-MPCD5%, respectively (Fig. 5a). The spectrum of CS/FG-MPCD5% also showed the occurrence of sulfur as ascribed to the binding energy at 166.85 eV. The C 1 s spectra of CS/FG and CS/FG-MPCD5% showed four slitted peaks, with the bonding groups of C-C/C-H corresponding to the binding energies at 284.82 eV and 284.78 eV, respectively (Fig. 5b). The other split peaks at 285.3, 286.4, and 287.1 eV for CS/FG and 285.6, 287.8, and 288.4 eV for CS/FG-MPCD5%, indicating the bonding structures of C=C/C-N, CO, and carbonyl (C = O) or O-C-O, respectively [57]. Additionally, the three split peaks for N 1 s spectra indicated the existence of C-N-C, NC, and N-H/ N–(CH3)3 + groups, attributed to the binding energies of 398.5, 399.3, and 400.8 eV for CS/FG and 399.6, 400.2, and 401.5 eV for CS/FG-MPCD5%, respectively (Fig. 5c) [57,58]. In addition, Fig. 5d displays that O 1 s spectra by splitting into three peaks at binding energies of ∼530.4, 531.8, and 532.1 eV for FG and 531.2, 532.5, and 533.1 eV for CS/FG-MPCD5%, respectively, validating the formation of C-O-C/C-O-H, CO, and O-C-O bonds [59]. Slight shifting in the binding energies of the characteristics deconvoluted splitted peaks between CS/FG and CS/FG-MPCD5% films, confirming the successful incorporation of MPCD as functional fillers into the CS/FG polymer matrix.

The elemental components were assessed using EDX spectra for the CS/FG and CS/FG-MPCD5% films (Fig. 5 (e & f). The results indicated that the primary elements in the film matrix were carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen in the CS/FG, while CS/FG-MPCD5% showed increased levels of sulfur. Carbon is constituted as the most dominant component. The CS/FG-MPCD5% films consisted of carbon at 50.16 %, which was higher than that of CS/FG films (49.57 %). The scans revealed that the elements were uniformly distributed throughout the surface, indicating that MPCD was distributed across the CS/FG matrix. Khan, Priyadarshi, Bhattacharya and Rhim [36] also observed a similar distribution of green tea CDs over the CS/FG film surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of MPCD-incorporated CS/FG films
ce:section.ce:para: CS/FG film also exhibited antioxidant activities, while MPCD-incorporated films showed higher DPPH-RSA and ABTS-RSA. MPCD-incorporated films (3 % and 5 %) showed almost 99 % ABTS-RSA (Fig. 6a). This scavenging effect could be plausibly due to the functionalized groups of MPCD localized on its surface of the films. These MPCD acted as free radical scavengers by electron and hydrogen ion transfer to neutralize reactive species from the oxygenated and amide groups in the surface of MPCD, thus preventing oxidative damage [60]. Moreover, due to their small size and large surface area, MPCD could easily integrate into the polymer matrix of the packaging film uniformly. As a result, MPCD could be released efficiently and contributed to the RSA. For the control film, antioxidant activity might be due to the carbonyl, hydroxyl and amide groups of biopolymers. Similar to other CD-incorporated films, the MPCD-incorporated CS/FG films showed ABTS-RSA comparatively higher than DPPH-RSA. This outcome aligned with the antioxidant activities of MPCD [20].

The antimicrobial potential of CS/FG film and those incorporated with MPCD at varying levels against EC and LM was tested using a real-time kill kinetics approach. The viability of the cells was enumerated every 3 h up to 12 h (Fig. 6b and c). The CS/FG film also showed prominent antibacterial action since CS possesses inherent antibacterial properties, which involve cell wall disruption, interaction with microbial DNA, and chelation of nutrients [61]. The films containing MPCD with increasing concentration of MPCD exhibited relatively better antibacterial efficacy and antibacterial activity. Notably, the film with 5 % MPCD showed high antibacterial activity, in which the growth of LM after 3 h of incubation was almost completely inhibited. Overall, MPCD incorporated films had better bacterial activity against LM than EC. The antibacterial activity of the films was primarily attributed to the antibacterial properties of MPCD (Fig. 2b). These results could be correlated with MPCD having a small size (8–10 nm), which favored the effective penetration into the cell wall, despite the thick cell walls of Gram-positive bacteria. The surface functional groups (hydroxyl and amine, etc.) of MPCD could promote electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged bacterial cell wall, aiding in the binding with the cell membrane. As a consequence, those MPCD disrupted the protein and nucleic acid synthesis and cell membrane permeability [39,40,62]. Additionally, MPCD may induce ROS production, causing lipid peroxidation, which damages the outer and inner membranes. These phenomena, promoted the intracellular components release, causing cell death [40,63].

The CS/FG blend greatly enhanced the overall performance of the film as indicated by the improved mechanical strength, flexibility, barrier properties, and antimicrobial and antioxidant activities along with excellent compatibility and homogeneous dispersion of MPCD. Basically, CS had the excellent miscibility with FG and yielded the homogenous film matrix, which favored the dispersion of MPCD throughout the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quality changes of minced pork packaged in active CS/FG films during refrigerated storage
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the pouch made from different active packaging films to prolong the shelf life of the packaged minced pork was performed against a sample packaged in a PE pouch during storage at 4 °C (Fig. 7 ). The pH of the minced pork samples packed in the MPCD-incorporated pouch had lower pH values till the end of storage, especially the samples packed in a pouch made from 5 % MPCD incorporated. The rise in the pH of the minced pork meat was due to the microbial breakdown of nitrogenous compounds associated with the more alkaline pH of the minced pork sample [64].

For the samples packed in PE pouches and pouches made from CS/FG films, the TBARS value reached 1.43 mg MDA/kg sample at the end of storage. The samples preserved in pouches containing MPCD had lower TBARS values ranging from 0.78 to 1.04 mg MDA/kg sample, which indicated that the pouches made from CS/FG film comprising MPCD effectively inhibited lipid oxidation. Among them, pouches made from films with MPCD5% had a more profound effect and these results were consistent with the antioxidant activity of MPCD (Fig. 2a). TBARS values are commonly used to determine the extent of secondary lipid oxidation products, and elevated MDA levels are linked with the rancidity of the products [65]. These observations revealed the potential applications of pouches made from MPCD-incorporated films for preservation of minced pork, which was particularly susceptible to lipid oxidation.

TVC and PBC of minced pork at refrigerated (4 °C) storage are shown in Fig. 7c and d. Initially (day 0), the TVC and PBC were 1.83 and 1.24 log CFU/mL, respectively, and steadily upsurged with rising storage time. Even after 15 days, the pork meat packed in pouches containing MPCD displayed values below the maximum allowable limit (6 log CFU/mL) [66]. The sample packaged in pouches made from CS/FG-MPCD5% films showed the lowest TVC and PBC than those packaged in other pouches.

Luminescent MPCD had the size in the quantum region and was compatible with CS/FG polymeric matrix, resulting in homogenous films with enhanced mechanical, hydrophobicity and UV blocking properties. The films also possessed excellent antioxidant and antimicrobial properties. The minced pork samples packed in pouches made from CS/FG film containing 5 % MPCD showed reduced rancidity and microbial count during 15 days at 4 °C. Therefore, the MPCD-incorporated CS/FG pouches could be used as active packaging, which could preserve minced pork or other foods susceptible to lipid oxidation and microbial deterioration. Nonetheless, the scalability of MPCD production, and variability in mango peel composition as governed by the variety and ripeness can limit the application of MPCD. Moreover, the optimization of MPCD production at industrial scale and the evaluation of toxicity of MPCD are still needed. Applications of MPCD incorporated films or packaging for various food products and their biodegradability should be further studied. Other preservation techniques, particularly non-thermal processing technologies, should be used in combination with MPCD containing packaging to increase food preservation efficacy.
